critic_raw:
The sufficiency criterion in fair selective classification is operationalized as a mutual information regularizer. Mutual information between predictions and sensitive attributes, conditioned on the ground truth label, is the target quantity, but estimating mutual information in high-dimensional spaces from finite samples is notoriously unreliable. The specific estimator used directly controls whether the regularizer actually enforces the criterion or merely correlates with it. If a neural network-based mutual information estimator (e.g., MINE) is used, it introduces a second optimization problem within the training loop that can have its own convergence issues, and the tightness of the bound on mutual information determines whether the fairness constraint is genuinely enforced.

Selective classification involves a model abstaining from classifying some samples — the coverage parameter controls what fraction of samples are classified. The fairness claim 'mitigates disparities in precision across the entire coverage scale' implies that the method works across all coverage levels, but the regularizer's effectiveness likely varies with coverage because the mutual information estimate is based on the accepted (classified) sample distribution, which changes with coverage level. A regularizer calibrated for one coverage level may fail to enforce fairness at different coverage levels without re-tuning.

The method is evaluated on four datasets, each 'consisting of a unique type of data.' If these datasets do not include cases where the sufficiency criterion is genuinely in tension with accuracy (i.e., datasets where enforcing sufficiency requires a measurable accuracy loss), the claim that 'the sufficiency approach may decrease overall accuracy in certain instances' is not empirically verified by the evaluation design — the cases where it matters most are not tested.

The baseline selective classification model used for comparison determines whether the improvement in fairness is attributable to the sufficiency regularizer or to the selective classification mechanism itself. If the baseline selective classifier (without the fairness regularizer) already produces lower disparities than a standard classifier simply through the coverage selection mechanism, the specific contribution of the mutual information regularizer is overstated.
all_issues_raised: Mutual information estimation in high-dimensional spaces from finite samples is unreliable; if a neural network-based estimator (e.g., MINE) is used, the tightness of the bound directly controls whether the fairness constraint is genuinely enforced or merely approximated.

The regularizer's effectiveness may vary with coverage level because mutual information is estimated over the accepted sample distribution, which changes with coverage; a regularizer calibrated at one coverage level may fail at others without re-tuning.
If the evaluation datasets do not include cases where sufficiency enforcement causes a measurable accuracy loss, the hardest claimed scenarios are not tested — the evaluation selects for easy cases.

The fairness improvement baseline is a selective classifier without the regularizer; if coverage selection already reduces disparities relative to a standard classifier, the contribution of the mutual information regularizer specifically is overstated.
